description:
You are on-call as an operations‐engineer and have been paged about sporadic performance
degradation on the host.  
Your goal is to collect a **30-second high-frequency snapshot** of four basic subsystems in
parallel (CPU, memory, disk-usage, and network) and then produce a single verification
file that the incident‐response automation can ingest.

Required file & directory layout
--------------------------------
1. Create the directory  
   /home/user/incident_001/metrics/  
   (create parent directories if they do not yet exist).

2. While the snapshot is running, write one line of raw text every second for exactly
   30 seconds into each of the following four log files:

   • /home/user/incident_001/metrics/cpu.log  
   • /home/user/incident_001/metrics/mem.log  
   • /home/user/incident_001/metrics/disk.log  
   • /home/user/incident_001/metrics/net.log  

   You may choose *any* command(s) that run without root privileges and give meaningful
   information for their subsystem (e.g. reading from /proc or standard utilities such
   as `top -b -n1`, `free -m`, `df -h`, `ss -s`, etc.).  
   The only hard requirements are:
     a. Each file must receive **exactly 30 newline-terminated lines**.  
     b. All four samplers must run **simultaneously** (i.e. they overlap in time, not one
        after another).  
     c. Stop automatically after 30 lines; do **not** rely on Ctrl-C.

3. When the 30 seconds are over, create the summary file  
   /home/user/incident_001/summary.log  
   with **exactly five lines, in the precise order shown below** (all uppercase keys,
   no extra whitespace, each line terminated by a single newline):

   CPU_LINES=30  
   MEM_LINES=30  
   DISK_LINES=30  
   NET_LINES=30  
   STATUS=COLLECTED  

   • The keys must appear literally as written above.  
   • The values must match the number of lines actually written to each corresponding
     raw log.  
   • `STATUS` must be the literal word `COLLECTED`.

What the automatic grader will inspect
--------------------------------------
1. Existence of directory  /home/user/incident_001/metrics/  
2. Existence of the four raw log files with **exactly** 30 lines each.  
3. Existence of /home/user/incident_001/summary.log containing the
   five lines listed above, in the correct order and with no extra characters
   before, between or after the lines.  
4. Timestamps or the actual raw contents are **not** graded, only the correct line
   counts and the exact wording/ordering of the summary file.

Hints (not mandatory)
---------------------
• Use background jobs or subshells (e.g. `&`) so the samplers run in parallel.  
• Utilities like `seq 30` combined with a loop around your metric command can help
  guarantee one-second spacing and deterministic line counts.  
• Double-check that each sampler has terminated before writing the summary file.  
• For counting lines reliably you can use `wc -l <file>`.

When you are done, double-check the summary file: the grader will fail the task if the
order, spelling, capitalisation or the line counts deviate even by one character.
truth:
Directory that must exist at the end:
  /home/user/incident_001/metrics/          (permissions 755 or more permissive)

Files that must exist at the end, all world-readable:
  /home/user/incident_001/metrics/cpu.log   — exactly 30 lines (any content)
  /home/user/incident_001/metrics/mem.log   — exactly 30 lines (any content)
  /home/user/incident_001/metrics/disk.log  — exactly 30 lines (any content)
  /home/user/incident_001/metrics/net.log   — exactly 30 lines (any content)

Summary file path and deterministic contents:

File: /home/user/incident_001/summary.log  
Contents (5 lines, newline-terminated, Unix LF):

CPU_LINES=30
MEM_LINES=30
DISK_LINES=30
NET_LINES=30
STATUS=COLLECTED

No trailing blank lines allowed.

The grader will:
1. `test -d /home/user/incident_001/metrics/`
2. `test -f` for each of the four raw logs.
3. `wc -l` each raw log and expect 30.
4. Read `/home/user/incident_001/summary.log` and compare the byte-for-byte content
   to the five-line block above.
